items.correct: Volume of air inhaled or exhaled in one normal breath at rest.
items.misconception: The maximum air your lungs can hold.
items.random_wrong: Air left in the lungs after maximum exhalation.
items.why: Students confuse tidal volume (resting breath, ~0.5L) with vital capacity (max breath, ~4-5L). Different lung volumes serve different purposes - tidal is the normal small ebb and flow.

items.correct: Volume of blood pumped by the heart per minute (stroke volume x heart rate).
items.misconception: The volume of blood in the heart at any moment.
items.why: Students confuse stroke volume (one beat) with cardiac output (per minute total). CO = SV x HR. A 70mL stroke at 70bpm = 4.9L/min cardiac output.

items.q: What is the rough formula for maximum heart rate?
items.why: Students misremember the formula. 220 - age is the classic estimate (rough; varies individually). A 20-year-old's estimated max HR is around 200 bpm; an 80-year-old's is around 140.
Correct answer: TV × respiratory rate. “TV + RR” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.correct: Maximum air exhaled after maximum inspiration
items.why: Correct answer: Maximum air exhaled after maximum inspiration. “Tidal volume” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: If TV = 600 mL and RR = 25 breaths min⁻¹, calculate VE (L min⁻¹).
items.why: Exact answer: 15. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
